---
title: "Generalization"
subtitle: "INFO 521 · Module 2 · Lecture B — Bias-Variance & Regularization"
author: "Greg Chism"
institute: "School of Information · University of Arizona"
date: last-modified
date-format: "MMMM YYYY"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import sys
sys.path.insert(0, "shared")

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import slide_helpers as sh

sh.use_slide_style()

ds    = sh.load_nhanes()
age   = sh.primary_predictor(ds)
sbp   = ds["y"]
age_z = (age - age.mean()) / age.std()
rng   = np.random.default_rng(521)

def mse(a, b):
    return float(np.mean((a - b) ** 2))

# Illustrative teaching signal (same as Lecture A): real SBP–age is ~linear and
# cannot show the bias-variance tradeoff, so complexity/regularization figures use
# a controlled nonlinear signal on the real NHANES age grid.
def true_g(z):
    return 120.0 + 2.0 * z + 15.0 * np.sin(1.3 * z)
y_ill = true_g(age_z) + rng.normal(0.0, 9.0, size=len(age_z))

# Ridge on a standardized polynomial basis (intercept handled by centering y, so
# it is never penalized). Standardizing columns lets λ penalize them comparably.
def poly_raw(z, deg):
    return np.column_stack([z ** k for k in range(1, deg + 1)])

def poly_std(z, deg, mu=None, sd=None):
    F = poly_raw(z, deg)
    if mu is None:
        mu, sd = F.mean(0), F.std(0)
    return (F - mu) / sd, mu, sd

def ridge_fit(Phi, y, lam):
    D = Phi.shape[1]
    w = np.linalg.solve(Phi.T @ Phi + lam * np.eye(D), Phi.T @ (y - y.mean()))
    return w, float(y.mean())

# >>> PLACEHOLDER — replace with the deployed week02 bias-variance/ridge tool. <<<
# → week02 bias-variance / ridge tool Pages URL after publish.
PLACEHOLDER_URL = "https://gchism94.github.io/info521-activities-2026/week02-bias-variance/"
```

## Learning outcomes {.smaller}

By the end of Lecture B you should be able to:

1. **Explain the bias-variance decomposition** of expected test error.
2. **State the tradeoff** and connect it to the U-curve.
3. **Derive and apply ridge regression**, and **tune $\lambda$ by CV**.
4. **Connect ridge to the Module 1 uniqueness condition.**

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: 2A found the U empirically; 2B explains it (bias-variance) and gives
a knob to move along it (ridge).
:::

## Recap: the U, and why {.smaller}

Lecture A: test error is **U-shaped** in complexity — underfit on the left,
overfit on the right.

Cross-validation told us *where* the bottom is. **Now: why is there a U at all?**

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: one line back to 2A. The U is not an accident — it is the sum of two
competing quantities. Name them next.
:::

## Bias and variance, intuitively {.smaller}

Imagine refitting on **many datasets** drawn the same way:

- **Bias** — the *systematic* miss: even on average, a too-simple model can't reach
  the truth.
- **Variance** — the *sensitivity*: a too-flexible model swings wildly from one
  dataset to the next.

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: this is the prediction-space view of the SAME repeated-sampling story
the Module 3 covariance-ellipse tool shows in *parameter* space — flag the
resonance. There, spread of ŵ; here, spread of the fitted curve.
:::

## Bias and variance on the data

<!-- ADDED: not in Clay source — illustrative nonlinear signal (see setup). Clay 14
     shows this repeated-fits picture on a synthetic signal; here it is the NHANES age grid. -->

```{python}
#| label: fig-bias-variance
#| fig-alt: "Two panels, each showing the true curve (vermillion dashed), 20 fits from repeated subsamples (thin blue), and their average (orange). Left, degree 1: the fits cluster tightly but the average line misses the curved truth — high bias, low variance. Right, degree 9: the average tracks the truth but individual fits swing widely — low bias, high variance."
#| fig-cap: "Repeated fits: degree 1 (biased, tight) vs. degree 9 (low-bias, high-variance)."

grid  = np.linspace(age_z.min(), age_z.max(), 200)
truth = true_g(grid)

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(11, 4.4), sharey=True)
for ax, deg, title in [(axL, 1, "degree 1 — high bias"), (axR, 9, "degree 9 — high variance")]:
    curves = []
    for _ in range(20):
        idx = rng.choice(len(age_z), 35, replace=False)
        c = np.polyfit(age_z[idx], y_ill[idx], deg)
        yc = np.polyval(c, grid)
        curves.append(yc)
        ax.plot(grid, yc, color=sh.OKABE_ITO["blue"], alpha=0.22, linewidth=1)
    ax.plot(grid, np.mean(curves, axis=0), color=sh.OKABE_ITO["orange"],
            linewidth=2.6, label="average fit")
    ax.plot(grid, truth, color=sh.OKABE_ITO["vermillion"], linestyle="--",
            linewidth=2.4, label="truth")
    ax.set_title(title); ax.set_xlabel("standardized age (z)")
    ax.set_ylim(95, 150)
axL.set_ylabel("response (illustrative)")
axR.legend(loc="upper left", frameon=False)
sh.finalize(fig, alt="Repeated degree-1 and degree-9 fits with their average and the true curve, showing bias versus variance.")
plt.show()
```

## The decomposition {.smaller}

For a fixed input $\mathbf{x}$, expected test error splits into three pieces:

$$
\mathbb{E}\bigl[(y - \hat{f}(\mathbf{x}))^2\bigr]
\;=\;
\underbrace{\text{bias}^2}_{\text{systematic}}
\;+\;
\underbrace{\text{variance}}_{\text{sensitivity}}
\;+\;
\underbrace{\sigma^2}_{\text{irreducible}} .
$$

The $\sigma^2$ floor is noise no model can beat. Full derivation →
[appendix-bias-variance.html](appendix-bias-variance.html).

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: expectation over training sets, at a fixed $\mathbf{x}$. The
irreducible term is why even the true model has nonzero test error.
:::

## The tradeoff {.smaller}

As complexity rises:

- **bias$^2$ falls** — the model can represent more;
- **variance rises** — the model chases the sample;
- their **sum is the U**, resting on the irreducible $\sigma^2$ floor.

[The best model is not the most flexible one — it is the one that **balances** the
two.]{style="color: var(--accent)"}

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: the U from 2A = bias² + variance (+ σ²). Underfit = bias-dominated
(left); overfit = variance-dominated (right). Regularization moves us left.
:::

# Controlling the tradeoff: regularization. {.center}

## Ridge regression {.smaller}

Add a penalty on the size of the weights:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{ridge}}(\mathbf{w}) = \lVert \mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\mathbf{w}\rVert_2^2 + \lambda\lVert \mathbf{w}\rVert_2^2 .
$$

- $\lambda = 0$ → ordinary least squares.
- $\lambda > 0$ → shrinks weights toward $\mathbf{0}$, **trading a little bias for
  less variance**.

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: the penalty discourages large, wiggly-curve coefficients. λ is the
knob that moves us along the bias-variance U — tuned by CV (2A).
:::

## Ridge: the closed form {.smaller}

The penalty just adds $\lambda\mathbf{I}$ inside the normal equations:

$$
\boxed{\;\hat{\mathbf{w}}_{\text{ridge}} = \left(\mathbf{X}^{\!\top}\mathbf{X} + \lambda\mathbf{I}\right)^{-1}\mathbf{X}^{\!\top}\mathbf{y}\;}
$$

::: {.fragment}
[$\mathbf{X}^{\!\top}\mathbf{X} + \lambda\mathbf{I}$ is **positive definite for any
$\lambda>0$** — so it is *always* invertible. Ridge **guarantees** the unique
solution that Module 1 needed full column rank for.]{style="color: var(--accent)"}
:::

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: through-line node. In M1, uniqueness required $\mathbf{X}^{\!\top}\mathbf{X}$
invertible (full column rank). Ridge adds $\lambda\mathbf{I}$, lifting every
eigenvalue by λ>0 — invertibility for free, even when $N<D$ or columns collide.
:::

## The coefficient path

```{python}
#| label: fig-coef-path
#| fig-alt: "Ridge coefficient paths: six standardized polynomial coefficients plotted against lambda on a logarithmic axis. At small lambda the coefficients take a range of nonzero values; as lambda grows, every coefficient shrinks smoothly toward zero."
#| fig-cap: "Ridge shrinks every coefficient toward 0 as λ grows (log-λ axis)."

idx = rng.choice(len(age_z), 60, replace=False)
zz, yy = age_z[idx], y_ill[idx]
deg = 6
Phi, mu, sd = poly_std(zz, deg)
lams = np.logspace(-3, 4, 60)
W = np.array([ridge_fit(Phi, yy, l)[0] for l in lams])   # (60, deg)

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
for j in range(deg):
    s = sh.series_style(j)
    ax.plot(lams, W[:, j], color=s["color"], linestyle=s["linestyle"], label=fr"$w_{{{j+1}}}$")
ax.axhline(0, color=sh.OKABE_ITO["black"], linewidth=0.8, alpha=0.4)
ax.set_xscale("log")
ax.set_xlabel(r"regularization strength  $\lambda$  (log scale)")
ax.set_ylabel("standardized coefficient")
ax.legend(loc="upper right", frameon=False, ncol=2)
sh.finalize(fig, alt="Six ridge coefficient paths shrinking toward zero as lambda increases on a log axis.")
plt.show()
```

## The two limits {.smaller}

- $\lambda \to 0$: the penalty vanishes — **ridge recovers OLS** ($\hat{\mathbf{w}}$
  from Module 1). Maximum variance, minimum bias.
- $\lambda \to \infty$: the penalty dominates — **$\hat{\mathbf{w}} \to \mathbf{0}$**,
  the model predicts a constant. Maximum bias, minimum variance.

The useful $\lambda$ lives in between — found by cross-validation.

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: both ends are degenerate. λ→0 is the overfit right end of the U;
λ→∞ is the underfit left end. CV picks the middle.
:::

## Choosing λ by cross-validation

```{python}
#| label: fig-lambda-cv
#| fig-alt: "Five-fold cross-validation error versus lambda on a logarithmic axis, for a degree-14 ridge model. The curve is U-shaped: elevated at small lambda (overfit), lowest at a moderate lambda, rising steeply at large lambda (underfit). A vertical line marks the CV-selected lambda."
#| fig-cap: "5-fold CV error vs. λ for a degree-14 ridge fit; the minimum is the CV choice."

n_cv = 90
ci = rng.permutation(len(age_z))[:n_cv]
zc, yc = age_z[ci], y_ill[ci]
deg, k = 14, 5
folds = np.array_split(np.arange(n_cv), k)
lams = np.logspace(-6, 4, 34)
cv = []
for lam in lams:
    errs = []
    for f in range(k):
        val = folds[f]
        trn = np.concatenate([folds[j] for j in range(k) if j != f])
        Phi_tr, mu, sd = poly_std(zc[trn], deg)           # standardize on TRAIN only (no leak)
        w, b = ridge_fit(Phi_tr, yc[trn], lam)
        Phi_val = (poly_raw(zc[val], deg) - mu) / sd
        errs.append(mse(yc[val], Phi_val @ w + b))
    cv.append(np.mean(errs))
best = lams[int(np.argmin(cv))]

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(lams, cv, color=sh.OKABE_ITO["green"], linestyle="-", marker="o", label="CV error")
ax.axvline(best, color=sh.OKABE_ITO["vermillion"], linestyle=":", label=fr"CV choice ($\lambda \approx$ {best:.2g})")
ax.set_xscale("log")
ax.set_xlabel(r"regularization strength  $\lambda$  (log scale)")
ax.set_ylabel("cross-validation MSE")
ax.legend(loc="upper center", frameon=False)
sh.finalize(fig, alt="U-shaped 5-fold CV error versus lambda on a log axis, with the selected lambda marked.")
plt.show()
```

## Live demo: bias-variance / ridge explorer

```{python}
#| label: live-iframe
#| echo: false
from IPython.display import HTML
HTML(
    f'<iframe src="{PLACEHOLDER_URL}" title="Week 2 bias-variance / ridge explorer" '
    'width="100%" height="430" loading="lazy" '
    'style="border:1px solid var(--border); border-radius:8px; background:var(--surface);">'
    '</iframe>'
)
```

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: the week02 explorer — slide λ, watch bias and variance trade off and
the fit stiffen. Swap `PLACEHOLDER_URL` (top of deck) after publish.
:::

## Explorer — static fallback {.smaller}

*The iframe above does not print or export to PDF; this snapshot stands in.*

```{python}
#| label: fig-fallback
#| fig-alt: "Static snapshot standing in for the interactive explorer: the U-shaped cross-validation error versus lambda curve with the selected lambda marked, the same view a bias-variance / ridge tool would show."
#| fig-cap: "Static fallback: CV error vs. λ (used in print / PDF)."

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(lams, cv, color=sh.OKABE_ITO["green"], linestyle="-", marker="o", label="CV error")
ax.axvline(best, color=sh.OKABE_ITO["vermillion"], linestyle=":", label=fr"CV choice ($\lambda \approx$ {best:.2g})")
ax.set_xscale("log")
ax.set_xlabel(r"regularization strength  $\lambda$  (log scale)")
ax.set_ylabel("cross-validation MSE")
ax.set_title("bias-variance / ridge snapshot (placeholder)")
ax.legend(loc="upper center", frameon=False)
sh.finalize(fig, alt="Static fallback snapshot of the CV-error-versus-lambda curve.")
plt.show()
```

## Forward hook {.smaller}

<!-- ADDED: not in Clay source — keystone-adjacent through-line to Module 4. -->

[The ridge penalty $\lambda\lVert\mathbf{w}\rVert_2^2$ is secretly a **Gaussian prior**
on $\mathbf{w}$: ridge is **MAP estimation** under that prior. We derive that in
**Module 4.**]{style="color: var(--accent)"}

So "shrink the weights" and "believe, a priori, the weights are small" are the same
statement — one more object doing double duty across the term.

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: plant this now. In M4, the Gaussian prior + Gaussian likelihood gives
a posterior whose mode is exactly ŵ_ridge with λ = σ²/τ². Regularization = prior.
:::

## Recap {.smaller}

- **Bias-variance** — test error $=$ bias$^2$ $+$ variance $+\ \sigma^2$; the U is
  their sum.
- **Ridge** — $\mathcal{L}+\lambda\lVert\mathbf{w}\rVert^2$, closed form
  $\hat{\mathbf{w}}_{\text{ridge}}=(\mathbf{X}^{\!\top}\mathbf{X}+\lambda\mathbf{I})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^{\!\top}\mathbf{y}$;
  always invertible.
- **Tune $\lambda$ by CV** — the knob that balances the tradeoff.

Module 2 has no standalone checkpoint; generalization discipline is assessed through
your modeling choices in **Project 1**.

::: {.notes}
Speaker note: bias-variance is the *why*, ridge is the *control*, CV is the *tuner*.
Carry the ridge-as-prior hook into Module 4.
:::
